{{Shrine |Name=Yamaguchi Daijingū |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |Founded=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name=Yamaguchi Daijingū |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |Founded=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{Shrine |Name=Yamaguchi Daijingū |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |Founded=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * revision.comment: 1 revision imported: attributing pages to myself
revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name=Yamaguchi Daijingū |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |Founded=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{ill|Daijingū|ja|大神宮}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yamaguchi Grand Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] {{Shrine |Name=Yamaguchi Daijingū |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |Founded=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{Shrine |Name=Yamaguchi Daijingū |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecrated=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{Shrine |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Shrine |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * revision.text:
'''Daijingu''' (大神宮)
== List of Daijingu Shrines by Founding Year == * {{ill|Tensho Kotaijingu|ja|天照皇大神宮}} (てんしょうこうたいじんぐう) ** Location: {{ill|Kasuyagun Hisayamamachi|ja|糟屋郡久山町}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Ino 604 ** Nicknamed "Kyushu's Ise Shrine." ** Tradition holds that it was enshrined by {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}, the consort of the 14th Emperor {{ill|Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}} and mother of the 15th Emperor {{ill|Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. ** Moved to its current location around 1677 by {{ill|Kuroda Mitsuyuki|ja|黒田光之}}. * {{ill|Hyūga Daijingu|ja|日向大神宮}} ** Nicknamed "Kyoto's Ise Shrine." ** Tradition states it was established by imperial decree of the 23rd Emperor {{ill|Kenzo|ja|顕宗天皇}}, enshrining the deity from the sacred site of Takachiho in Hyūga (modern-day {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|宮崎県}}). * {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} ** The Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. ** The system of {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|式年遷宮}} was established in the 14th year of Emperor {{ill|Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685), with the first ceremony held in the 4th year of Emperor {{ill|Jitō|ja|持統天皇}} (690). * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} ** The Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. ** The first {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|式年遷宮}} was held in the 6th year of Emperor Jitō (692). * {{ill|Yamaguchi Daijingu|ja|山口大神宮}} ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of the West." ** Founded in 1520 (Eishō 17) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshioki|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the Ōuchi clan, who enshrined the deity of Ise Grand Shrine. * {{ill|Isehara Daijingu|ja|伊勢原大神宮}} ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of Kanto." ** Enshrined around 1620 by Yamada Soemon and others. There is no record of an imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Gomizunoo|ja|後水尾天皇}}. * {{ill|Kaiseizan Daijingu|ja|開成山大神宮}} ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of Tohoku." ** Authorized by the Shrine Bureau in 1876 through the efforts of Chūjō Masatsune. * {{ill|Tokyo Daijingu|ja|東京大神宮}} ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of Tokyo." ** Established in 1880 as a remote worship hall of the Inner Shrine of Ise. Burned down in the 1923 {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}} and relocated to {{ill|Iidabashi|ja|飯田橋}} in 1928. * {{ill|Iseyama Kōtai Jingu|ja|伊勢山皇大神宮}} ** Location: 64 Miyazaki-chō, Nishi-ku, Yokohama, Kanagawa Prefecture ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of Kanto." ** Relocated to its current site in April 1870. Designated the principal shrine of Kanagawa Prefecture by the Meiji government in December 1870. * Tensho Kotaijingu (てんしょうこうたいじんぐう) ** Location: Ida Nakanomachi, Nakahara-ku, Kawasaki City, Kanagawa Prefecture * Tensho Kotaijingu (てんしょうこうたいじんぐう) ** Location: Oaza Kurakake, Azakamiwara, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture
== Other == * {{ill|Daijingu (Sakuragawa)|ja|大神宮 (桜川市)}} * {{ill|Daijingu (Tateyama)|ja|大神宮 (館山市)}} - A place name in {{ill|Tateyama, Chiba Prefecture|ja|館山市}}, derived from the local {{ill|Awa Shrine|ja|安房神社}}.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Jingu|ja|神宮}} {{aimai}}

itō (692). * {{ill|Yamaguchi Daijingu|ja|山口大神宮}} ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of the West." ** Founded in 1520 (Eishō 17) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshioki|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the Ōuchi clan, who enshrined the deity of Ise Grand Shrine. * {{ill|Isehara Daijingu|ja|伊勢原大神宮}} ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of Kanto." ** Enshrined around 1620 by Yamada Soemon and others. There is no record of an imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Gomizunoo|ja|後水尾天皇}}. * {{ill|Kaiseizan Daijingu|ja|開成山大神宮}} ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of Tohoku." ** Authorized by the Shrine Bureau in 1876 through the efforts of Chūjō Masatsune. * {{ill|Tokyo Daijingu|ja|東京大神宮}} ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of Tokyo." ** Established in 1880 as a remote worship hall of the Inner Shrine of Ise. Burned down in the 1923 {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}} and relocated to {{ill|Iidabashi|ja|飯田橋}} in 1928. * {{ill|Iseyama Kōtai Jingu|ja|伊勢山皇大神宮}} ** Location: 64 Miyazaki-chō, Nishi-ku, Yokohama, Kanagawa Prefecture ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of Kanto." ** Relocated to its current site in April 1870. Designated the principal shrine of Kanagawa Prefecture by the Meiji government in December 1870. * Tensho Kotaijingu (てんしょうこうたいじんぐう) ** Location: Ida Nakanomachi, Nakahara-ku, Kawasaki City, Kanagawa Prefecture * Tensho Kotaijingu (てんしょうこうたいじんぐう) ** Location: Oaza Kurakake, Azakamiwara, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture
== Other == * {{ill|Daijingu (Sakuragawa)|ja|大神宮 (桜川市)}} * {{ill|Daijingu (Tateyama)|ja|大神宮 (館山市)}} - A place name in {{ill|Tateyama, Chiba Prefecture|ja|館山市}}, derived from the local {{ill|Awa Shrine|ja|安房神社}}.

revision.text:
{{Shrine |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{Shrine |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * itō (692). * {{ill|Yamaguchi Daijingu|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:山口大神宮]] ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of the West." ** Founded in 1520 (Eishō 17) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshioki|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:大内義興]], the 30th head of the Ōuchi clan, who enshrined the deity of Ise Grand Shrine. * {{ill|Isehara Daijingu|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:伊勢原大神宮]] ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of Kanto." ** Enshrined around 1620 by Yamada Soemon and others. There is no record of an imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Gomizunoo|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:後水尾天皇]]. * {{ill|Kaiseizan Daijingu|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:開成山大神宮]] ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of Tohoku." ** Authorized by the Shrine Bureau in 1876 through the efforts of Chūjō Masatsune. * {{ill|Tokyo Daijingu|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:東京大神宮]] ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of Tokyo." ** Established in 1880 as a remote worship hall of the Inner Shrine of Ise. Burned down in the 1923 {{ill|Great Kanto Earthquake|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:関東大震災]] and relocated to {{ill|Iidabashi|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:飯田橋]] in 1928. * {{ill|Iseyama Kōtai Jingu|ja|伊勢山皇大神宮|zh|伊勢山皇大神宮|fr|Iseyama Kotai Jingu|en}} * {{ill|Daijingū|ja|大神宮}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:伊勢山皇大神宮]] ** Location: 64 Miyazaki-chō, Nishi-ku, Yokohama, Kanagawa Prefecture ** Nicknamed "The Ise Shrine of Kanto." ** Relocated to its current site in April 1870. Designated the principal shrine of Kanagawa Prefecture by the Meiji government in December 1870. * Tensho Kotaijingu (てんしょうこうたいじんぐう) ** Location: Ida Nakanomachi, Nakahara-ku, Kawasaki City, Kanagawa Prefecture * Tensho Kotaijingu (てんしょうこうたいじんぐう) ** Location: Oaza Kurakake, Azakamiwara, Tōmi City, Nagano Prefecture
== Other == * {{ill|Daijingu (Sakuragawa)|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:大神宮 (桜川市)]] * {{ill|Daijingu (Tateyama)|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:大神宮 (館山市)]] - A place name in {{ill|Tateyama, Chiba Prefecture|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:館山市]], derived from the local {{ill|Awa Shrine|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:安房神社]].

revision.text:
{{Shrine |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{Shrine |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{Shrine |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{Shrine |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} *
