{{Shrine |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |Deities={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |deity={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |deity={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |deity={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |deity={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |deity={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |deity={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |deity={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |deity={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |deity={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |deity={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |deity={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村 神明社}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} == External Links == * [http://www.yamaguchi-daijingu.or.jp/ Official site of Yamaguchi Daijingū] == See also == * {{ill|Shinmei Shrine|ja|神明神社}} * {{ill|Daijingū|ja|大神宮}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yamaguchi Grand Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Yamaguchi Daijingū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamaguchidaijingu.JPG|260px]] |location=Takimachi 4-4, Yamaguchi City, Yamaguchi Prefecture |LatDeg = 34 |LatMin = 11 |LatSec = 5.2 | N = N |LonDeg = 131 |LonMin = 28 |LonSec = 5 | E = E |MapCountryCode = JP |deity={{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}
{{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}} |ShrineRank=Prefectural Shrine |consecration_year=[[Eishō]] 17 (1520) |MainHall={{ill|Shimmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |Alias=Takanemine Shinmei, Takanemine Daijingū, Takanemine Jinja, Imaise, Nishi no O-Ise-sama |Map=Japan Yamaguchi }} '''Yamaguchi Daijingū''' (山口大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Yamaguchi City|ja|山口市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture. It was formerly designated as a {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|県社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == History == Yamaguchi Daijingū was founded in [[Eishō]] 17 (1520) by {{ill|Ōuchi Yoshitaka|ja|大内義興}}, the 30th head of the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}. The shrine enshrines the deities of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, specifically {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. In Eishō 5 (1508), Ōuchi Yoshitaka supported the former shogun {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|ja|足利義稙}} and accompanied him to Kyoto. During his 11-year stay in the capital, Yoshitaka visited Ise Grand Shrine in Eishō 11 (1514). After returning to Yamaguchi in Eishō 15 (1518), he sought and received imperial permission from {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|ja|後柏原天皇}} to enshrine the deities of Ise in Yamaguchi. Construction of the shrine began at the eastern base of Mount Takamine, with the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} completed in Eishō 16 (1519) and the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|内宮}} in Eishō 17 (1520). On June 29 of the same year, the deities were ceremonially transferred from Ise by priest Takamukai Nito Daibu Mitsusada. Initially called '''Takamine Shinmei''', the shrine later received the imperial titles '''Takamine Daijingū''' and '''Imaise'''. In its early years, the shrine conducted {{ill|Sengū|lt=Shikinen-sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}, or periodic rebuilding, similar to Ise Grand Shrine. However, as conflicts and financial constraints arose, this practice was modified or postponed. During the Edo period, the spread of {{ill|Ise faith|ja|伊勢信仰}} drew numerous pilgrims from western Japan to the shrine. It was known as "Nishi no O-Ise-sama" (Western Ise Shrine). Many stone monuments commemorating pilgrimages still stand along the approach. The shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine in 1928, renamed '''Takamine Shrine''', and finally adopted its current name, Yamaguchi Daijingū, in 1947. == Grounds == The shrine complex includes both an Inner Shrine and an Outer Shrine, along with associated subsidiary and auxiliary shrines. The buildings replicate the layout and architectural style of Ise Grand Shrine. * Inner Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Amaterasu-Ōmikami|ja|天照大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|ja|天手力男命}}, {{ill|Tamanoya-no-Mikoto|ja|萬幡豊秋津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}} – Deities: {{ill|Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|天照大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Haraedo no Ōkami|ja|瀬織津姫命}}. ** Inner Shrine Subordinate Shrine: Ebisu Shrine – deity: {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|ja|事代主命}}. * Outer Shrine – Main deity: {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大御神}}. Additional deities: {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦彦火瓊々芸命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futodama|ja|天太玉命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Yasakatome|ja|八坂刀自命}}. ** Outer Shrine Auxiliary Shrine: Taga Shrine – Deities: {{ill|Toyouke-hime|lt=Toyouke-Ōmikami (Aramitama)|ja|豊受大御神荒魂}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|ja|伊吹戸主神}}. Additionally, the precincts feature Taga Shrine and Takamine Inari Shrine, along with Iwato Shrine, located on the mountainside of Mount Kōno. == Annual Events == Initially, Yamaguchi Daijingū performed Shikinen-sengu every 20 years, mirroring Ise Grand Shrine. Although interrupted by wars and financial constraints, the practice resumed intermittently in the modern era. Major reconstructions occurred in 1880 and 1902. The most recent Shikinen-sengu was held in 2000. == Access == * 5 minutes by bus from {{ill|Yamaguchi Station (Yamaguchi)|ja|山口駅}} to Kenchō-mae Bus Stop, then a 5-minute walk. * Approximately 25 minutes on foot from Yamaguchi Station. == References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻[[新編相模国風土記稿]]2巻之46村里部大住郡巻之5|chapter=糟屋庄 伊勢原村 神明社|id={{NDLJP|1179219/19}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 伊勢原村
